title: Prioritize error recovery over decoration
summary: AI-generated error pages prioritize decorative elements (large error codes, whimsical illustrations) over recovery guidance, delaying the user's ability to resolve the problem.
instruction: Design error states to guide the user toward recovery first. Replace large error codes with plain-language explanations of what happened and what to do next. Use illustrations only when they support (not replace) recovery information.
rationale: The decoration-first error page pattern prioritizes visual design over user needs. Large HTTP status codes are developer-oriented, not user-oriented. The whimsical illustration creates a tone mismatch for serious errors (payment failure, account lockout), eroding user trust.
violations: HTTP status code displayed in large type (72px+) as the primary visual element
Whimsical or abstract 'broken' illustration (disconnected plug, broken robot, empty box)
Apologetic tone ('Oops!', 'Something went wrong') without specific guidance
'Go back home' as the only recovery action, regardless of error type
Same error page format for 401, 403, 404, 500, and timeout errors
controls: Recovery-first error page: what happened, why it happened, what to do next
Context-specific recovery options (retry, contact support, return to saved work)
Error communicated inline within the affected component instead of full-page takeover
Tone matching the error severity (serious for 500, light for 404)
borderlineCases: 404 pages on content sites where the primary goal is keeping users on-site
contextualExceptions: Consumer apps where a lighthearted error tone matches brand personality
detectorSignal: A large numeric code (3-4 digits) centered on a page with an illustration and a single 'Go back' or 'Home' link.
matcherProposal: Detect pages where a 3-digit number larger than all surrounding text is the primary visual element, accompanied by an illustration and a single recovery link.
detectorLimitations: Cannot distinguish intentional recovery design from decorativs pattern
falsePositiveScenarios: Deliberately branded error pages with known recovery paths
mitigationStrategy: Flag as a warning when the error code is the dominant visual element and recovery is a single secondary action.

fixturePlans.violation: 404 page with 120px '404', disconnected-plug illustration, 'Oops! Page not found', 'Go back home' button.
fixturePlans.control: Context-aware error: 'The page you were looking for (Pricing) was moved. Here are similar pages.' with search and navigation links.
fixturePlans.borderline: 404 page on a portfolio site where whimsical illustration is the brand.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
